content:
# 1 John Chapter 3 — The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Codex Sinaiticus (א), 4th century CE | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/1JN_002_page_001.jpg, 1JN_003_page_001.jpg

1. See what kind of love the Father has given to us, that we should be called children of God; and so we are. The reason why the world does not know us is that it did not know him.

2. Beloved, we are God's children now, and what we will be has not yet appeared; but we know that when he appears we shall be like him, because we shall see him as he is.

3. And everyone who thus hopes in him purifies himself as he is pure.

4. Everyone who makes a practice of sinning also practices lawlessness; sin is lawlessness.

5. You know that he appeared in order to take away sins, and in him there is no sin.

6. No one who abides in him keeps on sinning; no one who keeps on sinning has either seen him or known him.

7. Little children, let no one deceive you. Whoever practices righteousness is righteous, as he is righteous.

8. Whoever makes a practice of sinning is of the devil, for the devil has been sinning from the beginning. The reason the Son of God appeared was to destroy the works of the devil.

9. No one born of God makes a practice of sinning, for God's seed abides in him; and he cannot keep on sinning, because he has been born of God.

10. By this it is evident who are the children of God, and who are the children of the devil: whoever does not practice righteousness is not of God, nor is the one who does not love his brother.

11. For this is the message that you have heard from the beginning, that we should love one another.

12. We should not be like Cain, who was of the evil one and murdered his brother. And why did he murder him? Because his own deeds were evil and his brother's righteous.

13. Do not be surprised, brothers, that the world hates you.

14. We know that we have passed out of death into life, because we love the brothers. Whoever does not love abides in death.

15. Everyone who hates his brother is a murderer, and you know that no murderer has eternal life abiding in him.

16. By this we know love, that he laid down his life for us, and we ought to lay down our lives for the brothers.

17. But if anyone has the world's goods and sees his brother in need, yet closes his heart against him, how does God's love abide in him?

18. Little children, let us not love in word or talk but in deed and in truth.

19. By this we shall know that we are of the truth and reassure our heart before him;

20. for whenever our heart condemns us, God is greater than our heart, and he knows everything.

21. Beloved, if our heart does not condemn us, we have confidence before God;

22. and whatever we ask we receive from him, because we keep his commandments and do what pleases him.

23. And this is his commandment, that we believe in the name of his Son Jesus Christ and love one another, just as he has commanded us.

24. Whoever keeps his commandments abides in God, and God in him. And by this we know that he abides in us, by the Spirit whom he has given us.

---
## SOPHIA's Council Notes

Chapter 3 moves from identity (children of God, vv.1-3) through ethics (sin and righteousness, vv.4-10) to the love command (vv.11-24). The chapter's defining claim is v.16: love is defined by sacrifice, not sentiment. The Cain reference (v.12) grounds the discussion in Genesis: hatred leads to murder, and has done so since the beginning.

**Verses 19-20 — "God is greater than our heart":** The chapter's pastoral center. When our hearts condemn us (as they will), God's knowledge is greater — and since God is love (4:8), his greater knowledge brings mercy, not harsher judgment.

---
## ⚠️ Disagreement Log

### v.1 — "See what kind of love" vs. "See how very much our Father loves us"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "See what kind of love the Father has given us" | idete potapēn agapēn dedōken hēmin ho patēr |
| LOGOS | "See what kind of love the Father has given to us" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "See how very much our Father loves us" | "How very much" for potapēn; verb changed |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. potapēn means "what kind/what manner of" — it asks about the quality of love, not the quantity. PNEUMA's "how very much" converts quality into quantity. The author marvels at the kind of love (a love that makes us children), not just the amount.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA converts quality to quantity.

---

### v.9 — "God's seed abides in him" vs. "God's life is in them"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "his seed abides in him" | sperma autou en autō menei |
| LOGOS | "God's seed abides in him" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "God's life is in them" | "Life" for sperma (seed) |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. sperma means "seed" — a biological metaphor for divine parentage. PNEUMA's "life" removes the generative metaphor. The author's point is that God's seed (generative principle) remains in the one born of God, making continued sin incompatible with divine birth. "Life" is too general — it could mean anything, while "seed" specifically evokes birth and parentage.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA generalizes the specific metaphor.

---

### v.17 — "closes his heart against him" vs. "shows no compassion"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "shuts up his compassion from him" | kleisē ta splanchna autou ap' autou |
| LOGOS | "closes his heart against him" | splanchna = inner organs/compassion → heart |
| PNEUMA | "shows no compassion" | Abstracts the physical metaphor |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. The Greek says "shuts the splanchna" — the visceral organs associated with compassion. EMET's "shuts up his compassion" is nearly literal but awkward. LOGOS's "closes his heart against him" preserves the body metaphor (heart for splanchna) while being natural English. PNEUMA's "shows no compassion" removes the physical dimension entirely — the author describes a physical act of closing, not merely an absence of feeling.

**Status:** Minor. Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

### v.20 — "God is greater than our heart" vs. "God is greater than our feelings"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "God is greater than our heart and knows all things" | ho theos meizōn estin tēs kardias hēmōn |
| LOGOS | "God is greater than our heart, and he knows everything" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "God is greater than our feelings, and he knows everything" | "Feelings" for kardia (heart) |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. kardia means "heart" — the seat of thought, will, and emotion in Hebrew/Greek anthropology. PNEUMA's "feelings" reduces the heart to emotions alone. The heart in biblical usage is the whole inner person — it thinks, decides, and feels. When the heart "condemns," it is more than a feeling; it is an inner verdict.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA reduces heart to feelings.

---

## Summary of Disagreement Patterns

| Pattern | Count | Description |
|---|---|---|
| PNEUMA converts quality to quantity | 1 | "How much" for "what kind of" love (v.1) |
| PNEUMA generalizes metaphors | 2 | "Life" for "seed" (v.9), "feelings" for "heart" (v.20) |
| PNEUMA abstracts physical language | 1 | "Shows no compassion" for "closes his heart" (v.17) |

**Overall pattern:** Chapter 3's disagreements center on metaphor. The author uses vivid, physical language — seed, heart, closing one's viscera — and PNEUMA consistently abstracts these into more general terms. The most significant is v.9: "God's seed" carries the birth metaphor that structures the entire chapter (children of God, born of God), while "God's life" loses this specificity.

---

*— SOPHIA, AI Translator, in council with EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA*
